source_metadata.title_raw: James R. Williams - I-19-237 - February 21, 2020 - San Jose

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 21, 2020

James R. Williams
County Counsel
County of Santa Clara
70 West Hedding St.
East Wing, 9th Floor
San Jose, CA 95110-1770

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. 1-19-237

Dear Mr. Williams:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding conflict of interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your questions are general in nature, we are treating
your inquiry as a request for informal assistance.²

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090.

Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

QUESTIONS

1. Are sector-specific exchange traded funds ("ETFs") required to be disclosed on a Statement of
Economic Interests (Form 700) as an investment?

2. Are the underlying holdings in the sector-specific ETF required to be identified on the Form
700? If so, how would the holdings be reported?

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-19-237
Page No. 2

3. When assessing whether a public official has a conflict of interest, should the official assess the
financial effect on the price of the sector-specific ETF (e.g., VDE), or each individual
underlying holding of the ETF (e.g., Exxon Mobil Corp.)? In either case how should the
material financial effect be calculated and tracked?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Yes. If the funds are not diversified as specified in Regulation 18327, but rather concentrated in
one industry such as "oil," "natural gas," or "coal," these interests in ETFs are reportable as
investments on the Form 700.

2. Yes. An underlying holding in the sector-specific ETF is reportable if the investment reaches a
value of $2,000 or more during the official's reporting period. The holding and the value range
it reached during the reporting period must be disclosed.

3. For purposes of disqualification under the Act, an official must assess the material financial
effect on the price of the sector-specific ETF (e.g., VDE) and each individual underlying
holding if the value of a holding reaches $2,000 or more. However, whether an official is
disqualified from any particular decision depends on the facts surrounding the decision and the
determination can be made only on a case by case basis. If an official needs assistance
determining whether he or she is disqualified from any particular decision, the official will need
to seek further advice identifying the decision and relevant facts.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

You are County Counsel for the County of Santa Clara ("County"). On behalf of the
County's employees, elected, and appointed officials who are required to file a Statement of
Economic Interests (Form 700) pursuant to Section 87500 et seq. and the County's adopted conflict
of interest code, you are seeking informal written assistance on disclosure and conflict of interest
disqualification requirements related to ETFs, including sector-specific ETFs held by Form 700
filers in your jurisdiction.

An ETF is a collection of stocks, bonds, or other assets in a single fund that is traded on a
national stock exchange. There are many types of ETFs, such as ETFs consisting of only stocks,
only bonds, international bonds, international stocks, a mixture of stocks and bonds, and sector-
specific ETFs. Similar to a stock, an investor can purchase one share of an ETF and trade the ETF
on the market throughout the day, and the price of a share may fluctuate throughout the day.

In the information you have provided, you state that many ETFs are index-based ETFs,
which are designed to passively track the performance of a securities index, like the S&P 500 stock
index. Sector-specific ETFs are a type of ETF that seeks to track the performance of a specific
industry or sector of the market, like energy, real estate, or health care, and may do so by using a
benchmark index. For example, Vanguard offers an energy-specific ETF (ticker symbol: VDE) that
has total net assets of $3.6 billion and comprises 136 stocks of companies involved in the
exploration and production of energy products such as oil, natural gas, and coal. As of September
30, 2019, the 10 largest holdings of VDE-which covered 68% of the ETF's total net assets-were
Exxon Mobil Corp., Chevron Corp., ConocoPhillips, Schlumberger Ltd., Phillips 66, BOG

File No. 1-19-237
Page No. 3

Resources Inc., Marathon Petroleum Corp., Kinder Morgan Inc., Valero Energy Corp., and
Occidental Petroleum Corp.

Because ETFs are designed to track the performance of a specific index or achieve a stated
investment objective, an ETF's holdings may fluctuate throughout the day. All ETFs are required to
publish their holdings at least quarterly. Actively managed ETFs, which seek to achieve a stated
investment objective by purchasing and selling securities without attempting to track an index, are
required to publish their portfolio holdings daily. Non-actively managed ETFs, such as sector-
specific ETFs, may publish their holdings daily, monthly, or quarterly and may do so after a delay.
For example, VDE publishes its 10 largest stock holdings 10 business days after the end of every
month.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest. In furtherance of this goal, the Act requires that assets and income of public officials which
may be materially affected by their official actions should be fully disclosed and in appropriate
circumstances the officials should be disqualified from acting in order that conflicts of interest may
be avoided. (Section 81002(c).)

Investment Interests in Diversified Mutual Funds and ETFs

Section 82034 provides a definition of the term "investment" under the Act however; the
definition excludes interests in diversified mutual funds registered with the SEC: "The term
'investment' does not include a time or demand deposit in a financial institution, shares in a credit
union, any insurance policy, interest in a diversified mutual fund registered with the Securities
and Exchange Commission under the Investment Company Act of 1940 or a common
trust fund which is created pursuant to Section 1564 of the Financial Code ..."

Additionally, Regulation 18237 provides:

"...the term 'investment' does not include a fund, including
an exchange traded fund (ETF), closed-end fund or fund held in a plan qualified under
Sections 401(k), 403(b), 457 or similar provision of the Internal Revenue Code
(qualified plan), that is substantially similar to a "diversified mutual fund registered
with the Securities and Exchange Commission under the Investment Company Act of
1940" if all the following are met:

"(1) The fund is a bona fide investment fund that pools money from more than 100
investors and invests the money in stocks, bonds, or other securities.

"(2) The fund holds securities of more than 15 issuers.

"(3) The public official did not influence or control the decision to purchase or sell the
specific fund on behalf of his or her agency during the applicable reporting period.

File No. I-19-237
Page No. 4

"(4) The public official does not influence or control the selection of any specific investment purchased and sold on behalf of the fund.

"(5) The fund does not have a stated policy of concentrating its holdings in the same industry or business.

"(b) For purposes of subdivisions (a)(1) and (a)(2), an ETF, closed-end fund or qualified plan is presumed to have more than 100 investors and hold securities of more than 15 issuers."

Pursuant to the specific language of Section 82034, the term "investment" does not include an interest in a diversified mutual fund registered with the SEC and any investments in ETFs that meet the criteria for being exempted from the definition of income pursuant to the guidelines in Regulation 18237. To the extent that the filers choose to invest in mutual funds registered with the SEC and ETFs that meet the requirements outlined in Regulation 18237, they would not have reportable investments under the Act. Also, for purposes of the Act's conflict of interest provisions, any interests they have in non-reportable mutual funds and ETFs are not potentially disqualifying financial interests.

However, if the ETFs do not meet the requirements set forth above, for example, if the ETFs have a stated policy of concentrating holdings in the same industry or business (i.e., renewable energy), the ETFs are reportable. Based on the facts provided, the ETFs you mention are sector-specific seek to track industries such as "energy," "real estate," or "health care." Accordingly, these sector-specific ETFs are reportable.

Reporting Investments on Form 700

Regarding the question of how officials should report sector-specific ETFs, we can only provide general reporting information. "Investment" means a financial interest in any business entity (including a consulting business or other independent contracting business) that is located in, doing business in, planning to do business in, or that has done business during the previous two years in your agency's jurisdiction in which you, your spouse or registered domestic partner, or your dependent children had a direct, indirect, or beneficial interest totaling $2,000 or more at any time during the reporting period. (Section 82034.) Thus, public officials are required to report holdings in sector-specific ETF's the fair market value of a holding reaches $2,000 or more during the reporting period.³ The official must indicate whether, during their reporting period, the value was below $10,000, whether it exceeded $10,000 but did not exceed $100,000, whether it exceeded $100,000, but did not exceed $1,000,000 or whether it exceeded $1,000,000. (Section 87206.) For example, if the value of a holding in a sector-specific ETF reached the highest value of $11,000

³ An example provided in the Form 700 pamphlet is based on retirement accounts but is also relevant to overall investment reporting as follows: "Earl James Jones has $6,000 in an individual retirement account with an investment firm. The account contains stock in several companies doing business in his jurisdiction. One of his stock holdings, Misac Computers, reached a value of $2,500 during the reporting period. The value of his investment in each of the other companies was less than $2,000. Earl must report Misac Computers as an investment because the value of his stock in that company was $2,000 or more." (See FPPC Form 700 Ref. Pamphlet, p. 13-15.)

File No. 1-19-237
Page No. 5

during the reporting period, that specific holding would be reported as exceeding $10,000 but not exceeding $100,000.

Disqualifying Financial Interest

A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or more. Section 87103.⁴ As mentioned above, an official must assess the financial effect of all decisions before the official on any interests the official may have. This includes the financial effect on the ETF, as well as the financial effect on the individual underlying investment if the value of the investment reaches $2,000 or more.

Although any interest an official has in an investment could potentially be a disqualifying financial interest, our office would need specific facts in order to provide a thorough analysis on whether an individual would have a disqualifying financial interest in any particular decision.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Katelyn Greene
Counsel, Legal Division

KG:aja

___________________________

⁴ See Regulation 18702.1 for when a reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on an official's financial interest in a business entity is material and when the small shareholder exception applies.

700? If so, how would the holdings be reported?
